attributes.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with a one week history of intermittent and cramping pain in the left iliac fossa. On further history, she denies nausea or vomiting and reports normal appetite. She has not experienced any genitourinary symptoms. She has no significant medical conditions and has never undergone any surgeries. On examination, her vital signs are within normal range and she is afebrile. Her abdomen is soft, with mild tenderness in the left iliac fossa, but no guarding or rigidity. A urine beta HCG is negative and a CT scan is subsequently performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: What does the axial CT slice show?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with abdominal pain that has been present for one week. Her history and examination are reassuring, however a CT is nonetheless performed.

On closer inspection of the scan, one can see that the kidneys are fused over the lower lumbar region - see arrow (Image). This is horseshoe kidney and is an incidental finding. It is very unlikely to have anything to do with her episode of abdominal pain. In today's medical practice of increasingly frequent use of imaging it is important to appreciate that incidental findings are common. Some 20–40% of all CT examinations will contain at least one incidental finding.

Horseshoe kidneys are the most common congenital abnormality of the kidneys, with an incidence of approximately 1 in 400. More common in men than in women, this type of fusion anomaly is often an isolated and incidental finding, however a significant proportion (approximately one-third) are found in association with other congenital abnormalities. They can also be seen with genetic and chromosomal syndromes (e.g. Down Syndrome). Compared to those with unfused kidneys, individuals with horseshoe kidneys are predisposed to the development of a number of complications including renal stones, pelviureteric junction obstruction, trauma, urinary tract infections and tumours (both benign and malignant).

Concerning the other answer options, there is no evidence of these on the CT scan. As such, none of them are correct.
learningPoints: Identification of horseshoe kidney on CT scan
references: O’Brien J, Buckley O, Doody O, Ward E, Persaud T, Torreggiani W. Imaging of horseshoe kidneys and their complications. Journal of medical imaging and radiation oncology. 2008 Jun; 52(3):216–26
